items.title: Use to-issues to split the live plan into component work items
items.description:
Type: planning slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source plan: dev-nexus-pharo-work-tracking-handoff.md, NOTES.md, CONTEXT.md, and target-state.md.

Goal: refine the seeded local work-item stores into component-owned execution slices that a launched coordinator can select without relying on chat memory.

Acceptance criteria:
- Every seeded component work item has an owning component, stable behavior-oriented description, readiness classification, blocker notes, acceptance criteria, and safe verification guidance.
- DevNexus core items are separated from specialization and live-runtime boundary items.
- Runtime/image work remains planning-only until an isolated runner, disposable runtime boundary, and cleanup plan are recorded.
- Target state is updated so the next coordinator cycle can continue from durable project state.

Verification:
- List ready dogfood work across components with the DevNexus work-item CLI.
- Generate a DevNexus target report and confirm the latest cycle facts are durable.

items.title: Harden coordinator result-file contract
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)

Goal: make launched coordinator result reporting harder to omit or mis-shape while preserving the boundary that DevNexus records facts but does not choose or supervise implementation work.

Acceptance criteria:
- The launch context exposes the result file path and the result contract a coordinator must satisfy.
- The command environment exposes concise required/optional result-field hints.
- Missing or malformed result JSON still fails the launch before recording completion.
- Documentation describes the durable result JSON contract and the helper affordance.

Verification:
- Run focused agent-launch tests covering context, missing result files, and malformed result files.
- Run the DevNexus build or broader check when feasible.

items.title: Add relaunch readiness decision from target reports
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)

Goal: make relaunch, stop, wait, blocked, and failed decisions explicit from durable target reports instead of chat memory.

Acceptance criteria:
- Target reports combine the latest target cycle record, automation run ledger, target stop policy, and relaunch policy into an explicit relaunch decision.
- The decision reports the latest cycle id, latest run id when present, eligible work count when known, and a human-readable reason.
- The CLI and native MCP target-report surfaces expose the same decision shape.
- Tests cover relaunch while eligible, stop when no eligible work remains, wait for active cycles, and blocked/failed reporting.

Verification:
- Run focused target-report tests.
- Run automation target-report against this dogfood project after the cycle record is updated.

items.title: Improve component worktree guidance for launched agents
items.description:
Type: documentation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)

Goal: document component-aware source and worktree conventions so launched agents can safely work across a multi-component DevNexus project.

Acceptance criteria:
- Documentation explains that each component has its own source root, worktree root, tracker, verification hints, publication policy, and relationships.
- Agent guidance says to inspect dirty state before editing, keep generated worktrees and branches component-scoped, and report progress through the owning component work-item service.
- The guidance makes clear that agent-launch mode supplies context but does not create implementation worktrees for the coordinator.
- The guidance does not introduce specialization-specific terms into generic DevNexus source/docs.

Verification:
- Run documentation diff checks.
- Run a specialization-term scan over DevNexus source/docs.

items.title: Split transferred plan into next component work items
items.description:
Type: planning slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source plan: PLAN.md, AGENTS.md, CONTEXT.md, dev-nexus.project.json, and target-state.md.

Goal: split the transferred DevNexus dogfood plan into the next component-owned work-item batch so future cycles no longer depend on the old DevNexus-Control handoff or chat memory.

Acceptance criteria:
- Use the curated to-issues skill against PLAN.md.
- Create or update component-owned work items across the configured components, not one monolithic issue.
- Each item includes readiness, blocker, acceptance, verification, and publication notes.
- Keep live-runtime items blocked until isolated-runner approval exists.
- Update target-state.md with the chosen next direction and any decisions made.

Verification:
- List ready dogfood work across components with DevNexus work-item CLI.
- Generate a DevNexus target report and confirm relaunch eligibility is based on durable project state.

items.title: Add coordinator subagent dispatch progress surfaces
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source plan: PLAN.md priority candidate 3.

Goal: make coordinator-owned subagent dispatch visible in DevNexus records without making DevNexus choose, supervise, or assign implementation work.

Acceptance criteria:
- A coordinator can record selected, dispatched, in-progress, completed, blocked, and skipped work item facts per component through CLI and MCP target-cycle surfaces.
- Recorded facts can include the selected agent profile, bounded notes, and component work item references without requiring chat memory.
- The agent-launch context exposes enough policy for the coordinator to obey maxConcurrentSubagents and available profiles.
- Documentation states that DevNexus records coordinator-reported facts but does not decide which subagents to launch.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Keep DevNexus generic; no DevNexus-Pharo, PLexus, launcher, or image-specific behavior in core.
- Do not create implementation subagents from DevNexus itself.

Verification:
- Add focused target-cycle CLI/MCP tests for dispatch/progress fields.
- Run the relevant DevNexus automation and CLI test files, then npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to origin/main after focused and broader checks pass.

items.title: Support component-scoped parallel worktree records
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source plan: PLAN.md priority candidate 4.

Goal: harden DevNexus worktree-parallel support so coordinators can prepare and report component-scoped implementation worktrees across multi-component targets.

Acceptance criteria:
- Worktree preparation and metadata clearly bind a worktree to one component, source root, branch, base ref, and owning work item.
- Generated worktree paths stay under the configured component worktreesRoot and reject unsafe paths.
- Target/run records can report component-scoped worktree ownership without collapsing a project to one repository.
- Documentation gives launched coordinators a safe workflow for using source roots or generated worktrees per component.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Preserve unrelated changes in active component source roots.
- Do not make agent-launch mode auto-create implementation worktrees; coordinators still choose when to create them.

Verification:
- Add focused gitWorktreeService, worktree metadata, CLI, and automation tests for component-scoped records.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Improve component-aware target completion reporting
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source plan: PLAN.md priority candidate 5.

Goal: make DevNexus target reports useful as factual completion reports for multi-component dogfood runs.

Acceptance criteria:
- The target report summarizes active blockers, per-component progress, selected work items, commits, verification, and publication decisions from durable cycle/run facts.
- Work item references include titles/statuses when those facts are recorded or can be safely resolved through configured local trackers.
- The report distinguishes ready eligible work, blocked HITL work, completed work, and skipped work without relying on chat memory.
- JSON output remains stable for automation consumers.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Report factual recorded or tracker-resolved state only; do not infer hidden implementation status.
- Avoid live external tracker calls unless a provider is configured and the operation is read-only.

Verification:
- Add focused target-report tests for multi-component progress, blockers, verification, publication, and relaunch decisions.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to origin/main after focused and full checks pass.

items.title: Add low-token agent-facing status and eligible-work commands
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source plan: PLAN.md priority candidate 6.

Goal: reduce coordinator discovery cost by exposing concise project status, target report, eligible work, work-item update/comment, cycle record, and agent profile inspection surfaces.

Acceptance criteria:
- CLI and native MCP expose a concise eligible-work listing grouped by component using the configured automation selector.
- Agent profile inspection reports coordinatorProfileId, available profiles, maxConcurrentSubagents, and launch safety policy without dumping the full project config.
- Existing work-item create/list/get/update/comment and target-cycle record surfaces remain compatible.
- README or agent-facing docs show the shortest recommended commands for a coordinator cycle.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Keep the surface generic and component-aware.
- Do not depend on the DevNexus-Pharo specialization adapter for generic orchestration.

Verification:
- Add CLI and MCP tests for the concise status, eligible-work, and agent-profile outputs.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to origin/main after tests pass.

items.title: Harden neutral work-tracker provider conformance
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) for provider contract and mocked API coverage; human-in-the-loop (HITL) for live provider credentials or project choices.
Source plan: PLAN.md priority candidate 7.

Goal: make GitHub Issues, GitHub Projects, GitLab Issues, Jira, Vibe Kanban, and local stores map consistently to neutral component work items without assuming one project equals one repository.

Acceptance criteria:
- Provider capability reporting clearly distinguishes list, create, update, comment, labels, assignees, milestones, board support, and board status support.
- Mocked provider tests cover neutral status mapping, labels, comments, component configuration, and credential failure messages.
- Missing provider features are reported as capability gaps rather than hidden runtime surprises.
- Any GitHub Projects or Vibe Kanban gaps are captured as provider-specific follow-ups if they are too large for this slice.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Do not require live external credentials for this slice.
- Do not create Vibe workspaces, sessions, executions, or workers for implementation.

Verification:
- Run focused workTracking provider/service tests.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to origin/main after mocked provider coverage passes.

items.title: Harden Codex and Claude agent profile policy schema
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source plan: PLAN.md priority candidate 8.

Goal: make agent profile configuration explicit enough for coordinators to choose Codex or Claude profiles and obey model, reasoning, sandbox, safety, and subagent-cap policy.

Acceptance criteria:
- Agent profile schema and validation cover executor, model, version or variant when configured, reasoning/intelligence level, command/args, sandbox or safety posture, and intended use.
- Agent-launch context exposes the normalized profile policy needed by a coordinator without leaking unrelated host detail.
- Invalid or incomplete coordinator profiles fail preflight with actionable errors.
- Documentation explains coordinatorProfileId, subagent caps, and profile selection as infrastructure policy rather than work supervision.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Do not hard-code provider-specific behavior outside profile normalization/validation.
- Preserve current user-local Codex profile compatibility.

Verification:
- Add focused automation config/profile tests and agent-launch context tests.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Shape production-quality DevNexus project template from dogfood evidence
items.description:
Type: follow-up planning and implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after the dogfood loop reliably advances ready component work.
Source plan: PLAN.md priority candidate 10.

Goal: distill this dogfood evidence into a clean production-quality DevNexus project shape without carrying forward historical staging-project clutter.

Acceptance criteria:
- The template separates generic DevNexus project state, component configuration, target state, skills, and agent MCP projection cleanly.
- Historical DevNexus-Control assumptions are removed or documented as migration-only evidence.
- The shape works for one-component and multi-component projects without special casing arity one.
- Migration notes identify what can be generated, what must be user-authored, and what remains local runtime state.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Wait until the current dogfood loop has exercised the component-owned work-item batch and target reporting surfaces.
- Keep generated/runtime state out of source templates unless explicitly intended.

Verification:
- Validate the template with project status and MCP refresh on a throwaway local project root.
- Run relevant DevNexus project scaffold/config tests.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to origin/main after template checks pass.

items.title: Add generic DevNexus plugin capability projection contract
items.description:
Type: generic plugin infrastructure slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source plan: PLAN.md Plugin Direction.

Goal: add a generic DevNexus plugin capability/projection contract so multiple plugins can contribute agent setup without replacing DevNexus or supervising implementation work.

Acceptance criteria:
- Project model and docs distinguish DevNexus core from additive plugins.
- More than one plugin can be configured for one DevNexus project without assuming one plugin owns the project.
- Plugin capability records can describe projected skills, MCP servers/tools, setup obligations, environment hints, and cleanup hooks for agent contexts.
- Coordinator launch context or low-token agent-facing surfaces report available plugin capabilities without component-specific terms in DevNexus core.
- Documentation states that plugins provide capabilities and setup policy only; the coordinator still chooses work, subagents, and supervision strategy.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Keep the contract generic; no DevNexus-Pharo, PLexus, launcher, or image-specific source behavior in DevNexus core.
- Do not run live services or external plugin setup during this slice.

Verification:
- Add focused config/template/CLI/MCP tests for plugin declaration and projection surfaces.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Add shared coordination status and handoff records
items.description:
Type: shared coordination tracer-bullet implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source plan: docs/shared-multi-host-coordination-prd.md.

Goal: add the first generic DevNexus shared-coordination surface with status and handoff records, keeping the API small and avoiding hard locks.

Acceptance criteria:
- CLI and MCP expose coordination_status and coordination_handoff with minimal required inputs.
- coordination_status infers current component, worktree, branch, base/upstream, dirty state, pushed state, related work-item handoffs, and likely next action from project config, Git, and the configured tracker.
- coordination_handoff can write a structured work-item-backed record with host id, optional agent id, status, branch, base ref, head commit, changed areas, decisions, verification summary, and integration preference.
- Stale handoffs are reported as advisory freshness warnings, not locks.
- Documentation states that Git worktrees remain the parallelism mechanism and shared handoffs are coordination facts.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Keep this generic in DevNexus; no DevNexus-Pharo, PLexus, launcher, image, or Tailscale-specific behavior in core.
- Do not require live external tracker credentials; use local and mocked providers for tests.
- Do not auto-merge or auto-resolve conflicts in this slice.

Verification:
- Add focused CLI/MCP tests for status and handoff.
- Add mocked Git/work-tracker tests for Windows and POSIX-style paths.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Add shared coordination integration planning
items.description:
Type: shared coordination integration-planning slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after coordination status/handoff records exist.
Source plan: docs/shared-multi-host-coordination-prd.md.

Goal: add coordination_integrate so either Mac or Windows can forecast and plan integration using shared handoff intent rather than local memory.

Acceptance criteria:
- coordination_integrate fetches configured remotes when policy allows and gathers related handoff branches for a work item, component, or target branch.
- It uses Git merge-base, merge-tree or a temporary trial worktree, and range-diff where useful to report clean merges, textual conflicts, changed files, and competing branch decisions.
- It reads recorded handoff decisions before suggesting merge direction or order.
- It produces a structured integration plan without mutating source unless project policy explicitly enables integration branches/items.
- Conflict output is concise enough for a coordinator agent to decide the next merge step.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Depends on dev-nexus shared coordination status/handoff records.
- Do not implement automatic semantic conflict resolution.
- Do not require Tailscale or live external services for tests.

Verification:
- Add synthetic Git fixture tests for clean merge, textual conflict, diverging decisions, and stale handoffs.
- Add CLI/MCP tests for the integration plan shape.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Choose shared coordination provider and transport for Mac Windows dogfood
items.description:
Type: shared project rollout decision slice
Readiness: human-in-the-loop (HITL)
Source plan: docs/shared-multi-host-coordination-prd.md.

Goal: choose and configure the real shared coordination backing store for Mac and Windows dogfood once the generic coordination API is available.

Acceptance criteria:
- Decide the first shared tracker/provider for multi-host dogfood: GitHub Issues, GitHub Projects, or a DevNexus coordination service.
- Decide whether non-protected handoff branches may be pushed automatically by coordination_handoff, per component policy.
- Decide whether Tailscale is used only for private MCP transport or also for a hosted coordination service endpoint.
- Record the portable meta-project shape and host-local override rules needed so Mac and Windows share project identity without committing local paths, ports, secrets, worktrees, or runtime artifacts.
- Update project configuration only after the chosen provider and transport are explicit.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Blocked on a human/provider decision and any needed credentials or Tailscale setup.
- Do not commit host-local paths, secrets, runtime ports, logs, or worktrees.
- Do not mutate remote trackers until the provider is chosen.

Verification:
- Before provider approval, static plan/config review only.
- After approval, run read-only provider status checks and one harmless handoff/status smoke.

Publication notes:
- Dogfood project-state changes may remain local if this root has no remote; DevNexus source changes follow direct integration policy.

items.title: Add external coordination request API
items.description:
Type: external coordination implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) for provider-neutral API and mocked providers; human-in-the-loop (HITL) for live provider posting policy.
Source plan: docs/shared-multi-host-coordination-prd.md external coordination extension.

Goal: add coordination_request so agents can ask external humans or agents for approval, feedback, a choice, or review through configured provider surfaces, then continue from durable responses.

Acceptance criteria:
- CLI and MCP expose coordination_request with minimal inputs: intent, optional question/note, and optional target.
- The tool infers component, work item, branch, commits, changed areas, verification evidence, and relevant provider object when possible.
- Provider-neutral request intents are approval, feedback, choice, and review.
- Provider-neutral statuses include waiting, answered, approved, changes_requested, timed_out, and blocked.
- The implementation can draft requests without posting when provider mutation is not approved.
- Mock provider adapters cover GitHub issue/PR, GitLab issue/MR, and Jira-style comment/read flows without live credentials.
- Responses are summarized back into neutral DevNexus coordination records or work-item comments.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Keep the generic API provider-neutral; provider adapters handle GitHub/GitLab/Jira vocabulary.
- Do not auto-approve, dismiss reviews, merge, or repeatedly ping external humans.
- Do not require live provider credentials for the first slice.

Verification:
- Add focused CLI/MCP tests for draft, post-when-allowed, read/summarize, stale wait, and provider status mapping.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to origin/main after mocked provider verification passes.

items.title: Choose external coordination posting policy
items.description:
Type: external coordination rollout decision slice
Readiness: human-in-the-loop (HITL)
Source plan: docs/shared-multi-host-coordination-prd.md external coordination extension.

Goal: decide which external provider actions DevNexus may perform automatically and which must remain draft-only for Mac/Windows dogfood and later shared projects.

Acceptance criteria:
- Decide whether coordination_request may post comments automatically to GitHub Issues, GitHub pull requests, GitLab issues, GitLab merge requests, Jira issues, and review threads.
- Decide rate limits or quiet periods for follow-up pings and stale waits.
- Decide whether provider credentials live only in host-local overlays or can be referenced by named secret providers.
- Decide how to identify external humans/agents and when to require explicit target reviewer identities.
- Record the chosen policy in project configuration or docs before enabling live provider mutation.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Blocked on human/provider policy decision and credentials.
- Do not post to live external providers until policy is approved.
- Do not store secrets in the shared meta project.

Verification:
- Static policy/config review before live provider use.
- After approval, run one harmless draft and one approved provider smoke against a test issue or pull request.

Publication notes:
- Dogfood project-state changes may remain local if this root has no remote; DevNexus source changes follow direct integration policy.

items.title: Materialize worker context bundles for component worktrees
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source discussion: generated worker context should fix subagent confusion without changing the meaning of Git worktrees.

Goal: materialize a DevNexus-owned worker context bundle inside each prepared component Git worktree while preserving worktreePath as the source checkout root.

Acceptance criteria:
- Prepared component worktrees get a generated support bundle under `.dev-nexus/context/` with at least `context.json` and `briefing.md`.
- The context records projectRoot, component id, work item id/title, sourceRoot, worktreesRoot, worktreePath, branch/base ref, target-state path, root AGENTS/CONTEXT/PLAN paths, and result/handoff expectations when available.
- The briefing clearly distinguishes read-only project context from writable component source and says the component checkout AGENTS.md remains authoritative for component source rules.
- The bundle does not copy or overwrite root AGENTS.md, component AGENTS.md, PLAN.md, or target-state.md at the source root.
- Generated context files are excluded from source control or live only in existing DevNexus support state.
- CLI/run-once tests cover Windows and POSIX-style paths and prove existing Git worktree behavior still uses worktreePath as the checkout root.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Keep this generic in DevNexus; no DevNexus-Pharo, PLexus, launcher, image, or TypeScript-specific behavior.
- Do not introduce nested workspaceRoot/sourceRoot semantics in this slice.

Verification:
- Run focused worktree setup, run-once, and execution metadata tests.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Project local skills into prepared worker contexts
items.description:
Type: implementation follow-up
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after the worker context bundle shape is available.
Source discussion: workers should not depend on globally installed skills; DevNexus already manages project-local skill definitions and agent-native projections.

Goal: make project-local DevNexus skills discoverable from prepared worker contexts without requiring machine-global Codex skill installation.

Acceptance criteria:
- Worker context records the project-managed skills root and selected agent-native skill projection paths.
- Worktree setup can link or copy selected project-local skills into generated worker support state when the worker agent runs with cwd inside the component worktree.
- Skill projection remains generated support state and is excluded from component source control by default.
- The design distinguishes `.dev-nexus/skills` as DevNexus-managed source of skill definitions from `.agents/skills` or other agent-native projections.
- Tests prove stale/missing projected skills are detected or refreshed without modifying component source files.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Depends on or should build on `dev-nexus:local-19` so the skill paths live in the same worker context model.
- Do not install skills globally or write into user-global Codex directories.

Verification:
- Run focused skill materialization/projection and worktree setup tests.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Allow plugins to contribute worker briefing fragments
items.description:
Type: generic plugin infrastructure follow-up
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after the generic worker context bundle exists.
Source discussion: plugins such as DevNexus-Pharo should be able to contribute task-specific agent briefing sections without replacing DevNexus or supervising work.

Goal: let enabled DevNexus plugins contribute bounded worker context and briefing fragments that DevNexus renders into generated worker support files.

Acceptance criteria:
- The generic plugin capability model can declare worker context or briefing fragments with explicit id, title, body, intended agents/components when needed, and provenance.
- DevNexus renders enabled fragments into the generated worker briefing/context without embedding plugin-specific vocabulary in core code paths.
- Fragment ordering and conflict behavior are deterministic and documented.
- Fragments are advisory setup/context only; they do not select work, launch subagents, or supervise implementation.
- Tests use generic fake plugins to verify projection into agent launch and prepared worktree context surfaces.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Depends on `dev-nexus:local-19` for the base worker context bundle.
- Keep Pharo-specific content out of DevNexus core tests and docs except as generic examples if needed.

Verification:
- Run focused plugin config/projection and worker context rendering tests.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Decide whether to add nested execution workspaces
items.description:
Type: architecture decision and prototype slice
Readiness: human-in-the-loop (HITL) after the simpler worker context bundle has been exercised.
Source discussion: a generated meta-workspace root with nested source/ checkouts may be cleaner for multi-component or plugin-heavy tasks, but it would change the current worktree path model.

Goal: decide whether DevNexus should add an optional execution workspace root containing one or more nested component Git checkouts.

Acceptance criteria:
- Evaluate real pain after `dev-nexus:local-19` and any needed skill projection work land.
- Document whether the current `worktreePath = source checkout root` model is sufficient for normal component work.
- If a nested model is still needed, define names and metadata such as `workspaceRoot`, `sourceRoot`, `componentCheckoutRoot`, and multi-component checkout layout.
- Identify every deterministic tool surface that must receive both workspaceRoot and sourceRoot: Git status, verification cwd, commit discovery, archive/remove, dependency links, coordination integrate, plugin setup hooks, and DevNexus-Pharo Codex worktree helpers.
- Produce an Architecture Decision Record or target-state decision before implementation.

Blockers and boundaries:
- Do not implement nested workspaceRoot/sourceRoot semantics until a human confirms the simpler context-bundle path is insufficient.
- Do not break existing Git worktree behavior.

Verification:
- Static architecture review and, if useful, a throwaway prototype outside source publication.

Publication notes:
- Dogfood planning state can stay local; source changes require a follow-up implementation item.

items.title: Let toolchain plugins project dependencies into worker worktrees
items.description:
Type: generic toolchain/plugin setup slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after worker context bundles, project-local skills, and plugin worker fragments landed in DevNexus 5895ea2.
Source finding: generated JS/TS worktrees have no local node_modules, so test commands such as Vitest are unavailable and npx may attempt package fetches. Workers should not have to discover or repair this manually.

Goal: let enabled toolchain plugins contribute simple generated-worktree dependency projections and environment hints so language-local commands run from the component worktree without global installs or ad hoc package fetches.

Design direction:
- Keep DevNexus core generic. Add a dumb declarative plugin setup contribution that can be lowered to existing worktree setup primitives such as dependency links, generated support directories, Git excludes, and environment hints.
- Do not make a combined Java/TypeScript special case in core. Use JavaScript/TypeScript as the first concrete toolchain plugin behavior; Java can reuse the same generic hook later for JDK/Maven/Gradle hints.
- For JavaScript/TypeScript, prefer linking an already-reviewed source-root `node_modules` into the generated worktree when lockfiles match or when the project explicitly accepts the projection. Do not run npm/pnpm/yarn installs unless dependency installation is explicitly allowed by project safety policy.

Acceptance criteria:
- Plugin capability/config can declare generated-worktree dependency projections with id, source, target, required/optional behavior, source-control policy, and a concise reason shown in worker context/briefing.
- Worktree setup materializes plugin-provided dependency projections alongside project `automation.setup.dependencyLinks`, excludes generated dependency support from component Git, and records the result in `setup` and `context.json`.
- JavaScript/TypeScript fixture tests prove a generated worktree can resolve local npm/Vitest-style binaries through projected dependencies without global installs or `npx` package fetches.
- Missing or stale dependency projections produce a clear preflight/setup result telling the worker what is unavailable, instead of silently falling through to package fetches.
- The implementation does not install packages, mutate user-global tool directories, or add TypeScript-specific source paths to DevNexus core outside generic tests/fixtures/docs.

Verification:
- Add focused tests for plugin-provided dependency projection materialization, Git excludes, worker context reporting, and JS/TS binary resolution behavior.
- Run `npm run check`.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus `origin/main` after verification passes.

items.title: Support related-component dependency projections in worker worktrees
items.description:
Type: worker setup/toolchain follow-up
Readiness: todo until local-23 dependency projection behavior is reviewed against component graph needs.
Source finding: dev-nexus-pharo:local-4 workers could not run npm scripts in generated/manual DevNexus-Pharo worktrees because package.json expects ../DevNexus, but the worker worktree had no sibling DevNexus checkout.

Goal: let DevNexus project worker setup project declared related-component dependencies, such as a sibling DevNexus checkout needed by DevNexus-Pharo package scripts, without opening arbitrary outside-source path access.

Acceptance criteria:
- Project config can declare a dependency projection sourced from another configured component by component id, not only from paths inside the current component source root.
- Worker setup materializes the related component support path into the expected relative location while preserving Git ignore/support semantics.
- Preflight rejects undeclared outside paths and missing required component sources with clear errors.
- Generated worker context reports the related-component dependency projection and its source component.
- Static tests cover the DevNexus-Pharo-style ../DevNexus sibling dependency without installing packages or running live services.

Boundaries:
- Do not loosen arbitrary path traversal for dependency projections.
- Do not install packages, run live services, or require Docker.

Verification:
- Focused DevNexus dependency projection/setup/context tests.
- npm run check when feasible.

items.title: Publish DevNexus scoped dogfood npm prerelease
items.description:
Type: packaging/publication slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready for metadata, dry-run packing, and registry publish when npm auth allows.
Source discussion: make DevNexus accessible from more computers without sibling checkout assumptions; use a scoped dogfood prerelease rather than a stable public release.

Goal: publish DevNexus as a scoped npm dogfood prerelease suitable for DevNexus-Pharo and other plugins to consume.

Acceptance criteria:
- Package metadata uses a scoped Evref-BL npm name and a prerelease version/tag.
- Published contents are limited to built runtime artifacts, types, README, and package metadata.
- npm pack dry-run shows expected contents.
- npm publish uses public access and the dogfood tag if npm auth allows; otherwise the blocker is recorded clearly.

- npm whoami / npm publish result when attempted.

items.title: Project MCP approval defaults into generated agent configs
items.description:
Type: implementation follow-up
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: automation approval prompts after MCP rename.

Goal: make DevNexus-generated Codex MCP configs include default MCP tool approval settings so automations and subagents do not block on trusted project MCP tools after project mcp refresh or worker setup.

Acceptance criteria:
- Project MCP refresh for Codex can emit default_tools_approval_mode = approve for configured trusted MCP servers.
- Worker/project-local generated MCP configs keep current server names such as dev_nexus and dev_nexus_pharo, never the retired naming.
- Existing generated config tests cover the approval default and server-name behavior.
- Documentation or agent guidance notes that automation profiles use Codex approval_policy=never plus MCP tool approval defaults.

Boundaries:

- Do not broaden unrelated app/plugin approval policy.

Verification:

- Run npm run check when feasible.

items.title: Add DevNexus-managed conditional coordinator loop
items.description:
Type: automation infrastructure follow-up
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source finding: Codex standalone cron can wake a process, but it is the wrong long-term owner for perpetual DevNexus work. It runs on a timer rather than work-item or target conditions, has a restricted permission model in this environment, and can create concurrence instead of coordination if multiple wakeups overlap. The latest failure showed DevNexus run-once could load and record launch context, but the nested Codex coordinator failed before work selection because it was launched from the restricted cron shell.

Goal: make DevNexus own the conditional perpetual coordinator loop. External schedulers may still start or keep alive a DevNexus process, but DevNexus must decide whether work is eligible, hold the coordination lock or lease, prevent overlapping coordinator runs, launch or adopt a coordinator only when appropriate, and record durable target/run facts.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus exposes a simple managed loop command or service mode, such as automation watch/daemon, that periodically or eventfully evaluates target-report and eligible-work before launching any coordinator.
- The loop exits, sleeps, or waits without launching when no eligible work exists, when backoff is active, or when another run owns the lock.
- The loop uses the existing target lock/ledger/backoff policy and records skipped, blocked, launched, completed, failed, and wait decisions in target-cycle facts.
- The loop can run as a user process, system service, GitHub/CI job, or thin host scheduler target without making Codex automation the source of truth.
- DevNexus supports a current-agent adoption path for restricted hosts: an already-running coordinator can adopt launch context, result-file contract, target-cycle facts, and subagent cap policy without spawning a nested model process.
- Command failure summaries preserve enough stdout/stderr tail on failures to diagnose launcher failures without relying on ephemeral temp files.
- Docs explain the preferred architecture: DevNexus owns conditional scheduling and coordination; Codex heartbeat/cron is only a temporary or minimal wake-up bridge.
- Existing process-launch behavior remains available for hosts that can safely spawn agents.

Boundaries:
- Do not make DevNexus choose implementation work; the coordinator still chooses and supervises work.
- Keep the API generic, not Codex-only, even if Codex cron is the motivating failure.
- Do not create Vibe workspaces/sessions/executions.

Verification:
- Add focused tests for no-work wait behavior, lock contention, backoff, current-agent adoption, result recording, target-report relaunch behavior, and failure-output diagnostics.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

items.title: Fix MCP component-qualified work item routing
items.description:
Type: DevNexus MCP bug fix
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Observed behavior: the DevNexus MCP work_item_get call with projectRoot C:\dev\code\dev-nexus-dogfood and id dev-nexus:local-27 resolves the root project work tracker, which is configured as legacy vibe-kanban, and fails before checking the component-local work item store.
Expected behavior: component-qualified work item ids such as dev-nexus:local-27 should select the named component and its configured work tracker, matching the CLI behavior for --component dev-nexus local-27.

Acceptance criteria:
- MCP work-item get/comment/update/status calls parse component-qualified ids before selecting a provider.
- Existing unqualified ids keep current behavior and default component/root selection rules.
- Focused tests cover component-qualified local ids from MCP-style inputs.

Verification:
- Add or update focused DevNexus tests.
- Run the focused test and npm run check when feasible.

items.title: Add current-agent coordinator adoption contract
items.description:
Type: automation infrastructure follow-up
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: dev-nexus:local-27 delivered the DevNexus-managed conditional coordinator loop in af0b300, but intentionally left current-agent adoption as a separate contract.

Goal: allow an already-running coordinator, such as a Codex heartbeat or another restricted host process, to adopt a DevNexus launch context without spawning a nested model process while preserving DevNexus target-cycle facts, result contracts, and subagent cap policy.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus exposes a simple current-agent adoption command or MCP flow that creates or reuses launch context and returns the environment/result contract to the current coordinator.
- The adopted run can record completed, blocked, failed, skipped, and verification/publication facts without using nested codex exec.
- The flow composes with automation coordinator-loop so restricted schedulers can wake DevNexus while a current agent performs coordinator work only when DevNexus says a run should proceed.
- Docs explain when to use process launch, coordinator-loop, and current-agent adoption.

Boundaries:
- DevNexus must not choose implementation work; the current coordinator still chooses and supervises work.
- Keep the API generic, not Codex-only.
- Do not create Vibe workspaces/sessions/executions.

Verification:
- Add focused tests for adoption context creation, result recording, target-report relaunch behavior, and CLI/MCP shape.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

items.title: Slice Codex app-server provider PRD into implementation work items
items.description:
Type: planning follow-up
Readiness: human-in-the-loop (HITL) until the PRD is accepted.
Source: docs/codex-app-server-provider-prd.md and local Codex app-server protocol discovery.

Goal: review the Codex app-server provider Product Requirements Document (PRD), then use the issue-slicing workflow to create bounded DevNexus implementation items.

Acceptance criteria:
- Confirm the product boundary: Codex app-server is an optional executor/thread provider, not a Codex App automation or heartbeat manager.
- Keep DevNexus as infrastructure only; the coordinator still chooses and supervises work.
- Decide whether the first implementation should include only thread/turn control or also MCP tool relay.
- Use the issue-slicing workflow after approval to create component-owned DevNexus work items for JSON-RPC client, agent profile schema, ephemeral thread launch, target-cycle facts, optional MCP relay, and docs.

Verification:
- Review docs/codex-app-server-provider-prd.md against the current DevNexus automation model and Codex generated app-server protocol.
- Confirm no implementation item is marked ready before the PRD review decision.

items.title: Add Codex app-server JSON-RPC capability adapter
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source plan: docs/codex-app-server-provider-prd.md.

Goal: add the bounded Codex app-server protocol foundation DevNexus needs before it can use app-server as an executor provider.

User stories covered:
- As a DevNexus user, I can see whether the configured Codex app-server surface has the thread/turn methods DevNexus needs.
- As an automation operator, I get clear capability-gap errors instead of a failed opaque Codex launch.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus has a small internal JSON-RPC client abstraction suitable for Codex app-server stdio or a mocked transport.
- The adapter can initialize, detect method availability for thread/start, thread/fork, turn/start, turn/interrupt, thread/read or equivalent read/list methods, and report missing capabilities clearly.
- The adapter does not assume automation, heartbeat, cron, or schedule-management methods exist.
- Tests use a mocked JSON-RPC app-server and cover initialize success, missing method/capability reporting, protocol errors, and transport failure summaries.
- No private Codex automation storage is read or written.

Boundaries:
- Keep Codex-specific code behind an executor/provider adapter; do not leak Codex protocol details into generic work-item, coordination, or plugin concepts.
- Do not require a live Codex app-server for normal test runs.
- Do not create Vibe workspaces, sessions, or executions.

Verification:
- Run focused adapter/capability tests.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Add Codex app-server agent profile schema
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source plan: docs/codex-app-server-provider-prd.md.

Goal: extend DevNexus agent profile configuration so projects can declare Codex app-server executor profiles without replacing existing codex exec profiles.

User stories covered:
- As a DevNexus user, I can configure Codex app-server as an optional execution backend.
- As a coordinator, I can inspect profile policy and know whether a profile uses codex exec or codex app-server.

Acceptance criteria:
- Agent profile schema and validation accept a Codex app-server executor mode with bounded app-server configuration such as connect/spawn mode, command, listen endpoint, ephemeral-thread default, and host-local safety hints.
- Existing codex exec and Claude-style profiles remain compatible.
- Host-local values such as socket paths, token files, or non-loopback endpoints are represented as local profile policy, not portable project requirements.
- Concise agent-profile CLI and MCP summaries identify app-server profiles without dumping secrets or full host paths unnecessarily.
- Invalid combinations, such as non-loopback endpoint without explicit policy or missing app-server command for spawn mode, fail with actionable messages.

Boundaries:
- This slice only validates and reports profile policy; it does not launch Codex app-server threads.
- Keep the model generic enough that other executor providers can reuse the profile normalization pattern later.

Verification:
- Run focused project config, agent profile, CLI, and MCP summary tests.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Launch ephemeral Codex app-server worker threads
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after the JSON-RPC adapter and app-server profile schema are in place.
Source plan: docs/codex-app-server-provider-prd.md.

Goal: let DevNexus launch a Codex app-server-backed worker thread or turn through the selected app-server profile, using ephemeral threads by default.

User stories covered:
- As a coordinator, I can request a temporary Codex worker for a selected work item and receive thread/turn identity plus run metadata.
- As a coordinator, I can start or fork a Codex thread with the intended cwd, model, reasoning, permissions, and project context.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus can start or fork a Codex app-server thread from a configured profile and start a turn with coordinator-provided input.
- Ephemeral threads are used by default when the profile requests them, with an explicit durable opt-in for visible/persisted Codex chats.
- Launch input includes the DevNexus context/result contract paths and respects selected cwd, model, reasoning, approval policy, sandbox or permission profile, and MCP approval defaults where the protocol supports them.
- The provider returns thread id, turn id, ephemeral status, cwd, model, and failure summaries to the caller.
- Focused tests use the mocked app-server adapter and do not require live Codex.

Blockers and sequencing:
- Depends on the Codex app-server JSON-RPC capability adapter.
- Depends on Codex app-server agent profile schema.

Boundaries:
- DevNexus still does not choose implementation work or supervise workers; the coordinator decides when to use the provider.
- Do not implement MCP tool relay in this slice except as required to preserve normal thread startup configuration.

Verification:
- Run focused launch-provider tests.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Record Codex app-server launch and target-cycle facts
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after Codex app-server worker launch is available.
Source plan: docs/codex-app-server-provider-prd.md.

Goal: integrate Codex app-server-backed launches with DevNexus target-cycle, launch, result-file, verification, and failure recording so app-server runs are as observable as command-based runs.

User stories covered:
- As an automation operator, I can run DevNexus coordinator-loop and see wait, launched, completed, blocked, failed, and skipped decisions recorded before and after app-server-backed work.
- As a project maintainer, I can diagnose Codex app-server failures from DevNexus records without relying on private Codex UI state.

Acceptance criteria:
- App-server launch records include profile id, thread id, turn id, ephemeral/durable status, cwd, selected model, result path, and concise stdout/stderr or protocol failure summaries when available.
- DevNexus does not mark an app-server-backed run successful until the configured result contract is present and valid.
- Failed, blocked, interrupted, and timed-out app-server turns are reflected in automation run and target-cycle facts.
- Coordinator-loop behavior remains consistent for no work, active locks, backoff, launch, completion, and failure when the selected profile uses app-server.
- Tests cover target-cycle/report behavior for app-server run success, malformed result, failure, and timeout.

Blockers and sequencing:
- Depends on ephemeral Codex app-server worker launch.

Boundaries:
- Keep current command-based run-once/coordinator-loop behavior available.
- Do not manage Codex heartbeat or cron automations.

Verification:
- Run focused automation run-once/coordinator-loop/target-report tests.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Optionally relay MCP tool calls through Codex app-server
items.description:
Type: implementation follow-up
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after basic Codex app-server thread/turn launch and target-cycle recording are stable.
Source plan: docs/codex-app-server-provider-prd.md.

Goal: optionally let DevNexus call Codex-managed MCP tools through app-server when a coordinator or worker needs a Codex-owned MCP server surface.

User stories covered:
- As a coordinator, I can use Codex app-server to call a configured MCP tool in the context of a managed thread when that is safer than direct process-level wiring.
- As a DevNexus user, I get clear MCP capability and approval failures instead of silent blocked automation.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus can list or check MCP server status for an app-server thread when the protocol supports it.
- DevNexus can relay a bounded MCP tool call through Codex app-server with structured arguments and receive structured content/error results.
- Approval, permission, missing server, missing tool, and protocol errors are reported as infrastructure failures with actionable summaries.
- The relay is optional and not required for basic app-server execution.
- Tests use mocked app-server MCP responses and do not require live MCP servers.

Blockers and sequencing:
- Depends on stable Codex app-server launch and target-cycle recording.

Boundaries:
- Do not bypass Codex MCP trust or approval policy.
- Do not hard-code DevNexus-Pharo, PLexus, or Pharo MCP server names in generic core.

Verification:
- Run focused app-server MCP relay tests.
- Run npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Document Codex exec, adoption, and app-server profile choices
items.description:
Type: documentation and operator guidance slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after the first app-server execution path lands; can start earlier as draft documentation if useful.
Source plan: docs/codex-app-server-provider-prd.md.

Goal: document when a DevNexus project should use codex exec, current-agent adoption, or Codex app-server profiles.

User stories covered:
- As a DevNexus user, I can choose the simplest Codex integration mode for my host.
- As an automation operator, I understand that Codex heartbeats are wake-up bridges and not DevNexus' long-term scheduling owner.

Acceptance criteria:
- Documentation compares command-based codex exec profiles, current-agent adoption, and Codex app-server profiles.
- The docs explain ephemeral versus durable Codex threads and when to use each.
- The docs state that DevNexus does not create or mutate Codex App heartbeat/cron automation records in the first version.
- The docs cover safety defaults for loopback endpoints, non-loopback policy, tokens/socket paths as host-local config, and approval/sandbox preservation.
- Examples avoid user-specific absolute paths except where clearly labeled as host-local placeholders.

Blockers and sequencing:
- Depends on final naming/config shape from the profile schema and launch-provider slices for non-draft docs.

Verification:
- Run documentation diff checks.
- Run npm run check when feasible if docs are in the DevNexus package.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Provide JS/TS toolchain support for generated worker worktrees
items.description:
Type: worker setup/toolchain follow-up
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source finding: the five-agent DevNexus wave on 2026-05-17 showed generated Git worktrees often lack local node_modules. Workers A and E could implement changes but could not run npm run build or focused Vitest inside their assigned worktrees because tsc/vitest were not resolvable.

Goal: make JavaScript/TypeScript DevNexus worker worktrees able to run configured project checks without ad hoc installs or unsafe registry fetches.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus worker setup can project or reference a configured JS/TS toolchain/dependency context for generated worktrees.
- The approach does not copy large node_modules blindly into every worktree unless explicitly configured.
- Worker context explains how npm scripts and focused tests should be run in generated worktrees.
- Preflight reports a clear blocker when the required dependency context is missing.
- Static tests cover a generated worktree using dependency/toolchain support to resolve tsc/vitest from an approved source.

Boundaries:
- Do not require live network or npm registry access in normal tests.
- Do not hide arbitrary outside-source path access behind the toolchain feature.
- Keep this generic enough for future language plugins, but implement the JS/TS case first.

Verification:
- Focused worker setup/context tests.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Add publication identity and remote guardrails
items.description:
Type: Git publication/account safety implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source discussion: the dogfood meta repository is owned by the dedicated machine user Gabot-Darbot while the human account Gabriel-Darbord remains the normal personal CLI/Git identity. Local setup uses plain gh for Gabriel-Darbord, gh-gabot with GH_CONFIG_DIR for Gabot-Darbot, origin for personal SSH access, and bot for the bot SSH host alias.

Goal: make DevNexus prevent GitHub account and remote mix-ups by modeling publication identity and validating remotes/accounts before push or provider writes.

Acceptance criteria:
- Project/component publication policy can declare the intended actor for automated publication or provider writes, such as a named machine user, GitHub App, or human account.
- Publication policy can declare the expected Git remote name, expected remote URL or SSH host alias, and optional command environment such as GH_CONFIG_DIR without storing secrets.
- DevNexus preflight/status surfaces report the effective Git remote, upstream branch, push URL, expected actor, and observed actor when it can be checked safely.
- Automation/worker launch context tells agents which remote/account is intended for human work versus bot automation.
- Before any DevNexus-owned push or live provider mutation, DevNexus can block with an actionable error when the configured remote or actor does not match policy.
- Documentation covers the recommended GitHub machine-user setup: SSH host alias for Git, isolated gh config for API calls, personal origin plus bot remote when both accounts need access, and no secrets in shared meta repos.
- Tests mock git and gh/app-token checks; no live GitHub credentials are required.

Boundaries:
- Do not hard-code Gabot-Darbot or Gabriel-Darbord into DevNexus core; use them only as examples in dogfood-local docs/fixtures if needed.
- Keep GitHub-specific details behind provider/adaptor policy so GitLab/Jira can reuse the generic actor concept later.
- Do not make DevNexus choose work or merge policy; this only guards configured publication/provider actions.

Verification:
- Focused publication/account-policy tests.
- Focused agent launch/context tests proving the intended actor/remote is surfaced.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Add meta-project hosting configuration
items.description:
Type: meta-project hosting configuration slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) for config/schema/docs and mocked GitHub provider behavior; human-in-the-loop (HITL) for any live org/account/repository provisioning.
Source discussion: DevNexus meta projects should be pushable/shareable through a configured GitHub owner/namespace, such as a dedicated bot account repository or a private organization. The hosting namespace, repository naming policy, visibility, default branch, and automation actor should be DevNexus-managed project configuration rather than ad hoc local Git setup.

Goal: add first-class DevNexus configuration for where shared meta-project repositories live and how they are provisioned, without storing secrets in shared project state.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus project config can describe a meta-project hosting target: provider, owner/namespace, repository naming convention, visibility, default branch, remote names, and whether DevNexus may create the remote repository automatically.
- The hosting target can represent either a user-owned machine-account namespace such as `Gabot-Darbot` or a private organization namespace, without changing the rest of the project model.
- Config separates portable hosting intent from host-local authentication material. Shared config may reference a named auth profile, but tokens, app keys, SSH private keys, and absolute credential paths stay in host-local config/overrides.
- DevNexus can generate or validate the expected meta repo remote URLs for human and automation remotes, such as personal `origin` plus bot `bot`, without requiring agents to choose manually.
- DevNexus can preflight whether the remote repo exists and whether the configured actor appears to have the required read/write/admin capability; live creation remains policy-gated.
- Documentation explains recommended setups: dedicated machine-user account repo, private organization repo, and later GitHub App installation, including tradeoffs for multi-host dogfood.
- Mocked provider tests cover repo-name derivation, remote generation, missing repo, permission mismatch, and host-local auth profile lookup failure.

Boundaries:
- Do not hard-code Gabot-Darbot, Gabriel-Darbord, or one GitHub organization into DevNexus core; use examples only in docs/fixtures.
- Do not require a real GitHub token or live repo creation in normal tests.
- Keep provider-specific GitHub details behind a hosting/provider adapter so GitLab can be added later.
- Do not conflate source component publication with meta-project hosting; source repos may remain in an upstream organization while meta repos live in a DevNexus-owned namespace.

Relationship:
- Complements `dev-nexus:local-38`, which guards the actor/remote used for publication and provider writes. This item owns where meta repositories are configured/provisioned.

Verification:
- Focused project config/schema tests.
- Focused hosting/remote-generation tests with mocked provider responses.
- Focused docs/example validation if available.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Add DevNexus guided setup assistant service
items.description:
Type: DevNexus onboarding infrastructure slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source discussion: users need hand-holding for DevNexus setup, especially bot/machine-user setup, meta-project hosting, auth profile setup, and bringing an existing project onto a new machine. A skill alone is insufficient because onboarding needs checks, generated commands, stateful progress, and safe automation.

Goal: add a generic DevNexus setup assistant service that can present guided setup flows, run safe preflight checks, generate exact user steps, and record progress without making setup depend on chat memory.

Feature choice:
- Implement this as DevNexus core setup/onboarding service exposed through CLI and MCP.
- Use skills/docs as projected guidance and explanatory material, not as the authoritative setup engine.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus has a generic setup flow model with steps, prerequisites, manual actions, safe automated actions, verification checks, generated commands, and next-action summaries.
- CLI exposes a low-token flow surface, for example `setup list`, `setup plan <flow>`, `setup check <flow>`, and `setup record <flow-step>`, with JSON output for agents.
- MCP exposes equivalent setup-assistant tools for agent use without requiring a separate plugin.
- Setup steps can explicitly mark manual actions, such as creating a GitHub account, browser/device auth, or accepting an invite, versus automatable actions like generating SSH config or validating remotes.
- Flow progress can be persisted in DevNexus project state or a host-local setup state file according to whether the fact is portable or machine-specific.
- The service can run in noninteractive mode and produce exact instructions instead of blocking on prompts.
- Tests cover flow modeling, check execution, manual-step rendering, persistence boundaries, and JSON/CLI output.

Boundaries:
- Do not store tokens, private keys, browser auth state, or absolute host-secret paths in shared project state.
- Do not hard-code GitHub-only behavior into the generic setup engine.
- Do not make DevNexus choose implementation work; this is project setup infrastructure.

Verification:
- Focused setup service/model tests.
- Focused CLI/MCP tests for setup flow output.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Add GitHub bot and meta-repo onboarding flow
items.description:
Type: GitHub/meta-project onboarding flow slice
Readiness: todo/dependent until the generic setup assistant service and meta-project hosting config shape are available.
Source discussion: DevNexus should hand-hold users through creating or choosing a bot/machine-user account, deciding whether meta projects live under the bot account or an organization, configuring SSH/GitHub CLI auth profiles, and creating/pushing the shared DevNexus meta repo.

Goal: add a DevNexus setup flow for initial project creation with GitHub-backed meta-project hosting and a dedicated automation actor.

Acceptance criteria:
- Setup flow can recommend and customize names for a bot account and optional org/namespace, such as `<user>-bot` and `<user>-dev-nexus`, while allowing custom names like `Gabot-Darbot`.
- The flow explains what must be manual, such as creating a GitHub account, verifying email, completing browser/device auth, and creating an organization if GitHub API permissions cannot do it.
- The flow can generate exact commands for SSH key creation, SSH host alias config, isolated `gh` config via `GH_CONFIG_DIR`, and Git remotes without exposing private material.
- The flow can verify plain user auth, bot auth, SSH host aliases, repo existence, collaborator access, and intended remotes.
- The flow can create or propose creating the private meta repo when policy and credentials allow; otherwise it emits precise manual steps and resumes from verification checks.
- The flow configures DevNexus project hosting metadata and publication guardrails so future agents know which actor/remote is intended.
- The flow produces a final setup report suitable for handoff to another machine or agent.

Dependencies:
- Depends on `dev-nexus:local-40` for the generic setup assistant service.
- Depends on `dev-nexus:local-39` for meta-project hosting configuration.
- Complements `dev-nexus:local-38` for actor/remote guardrails.

Boundaries:
- Do not automate GitHub account creation; GitHub account creation remains a manual step.
- Do not store secrets in the shared meta repo.
- Do not force an organization; support bot-owned repos and org-owned repos.

Verification:
- Mocked GitHub/gh/SSH checks for happy path and missing-permission paths.
- Static docs/example checks.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after dependencies and verification pass.

items.title: Add new-machine setup flow for existing DevNexus projects
items.description:
Type: existing-project/new-machine onboarding flow slice
Readiness: todo/dependent until the generic setup assistant service and meta-project hosting/auth-profile config shape are available.
Source discussion: users should be able to bring a shared DevNexus project onto another machine, such as Mac plus Windows, without re-discovering bot account setup, remotes, component roots, MCP refresh, skills projection, and local toolchain dependencies by hand.

Goal: add a DevNexus setup flow for joining an existing shared DevNexus meta project on a new machine.

Acceptance criteria:
- Flow starts from a meta repo URL or known hosting config and guides clone/fetch of the DevNexus meta project into a local root.
- Flow identifies portable project state versus host-local state and prompts/generates missing host-local overrides for source roots, worktree roots, auth profiles, MCP config, skills projection, runtime/state roots, and toolchain paths.
- Flow verifies personal and bot/API auth profiles according to project policy, including plain user `gh`, bot wrapper or app profile, SSH host aliases, and remote access.
- Flow verifies component source checkouts: clone if absent when policy allows, fetch/pull if present, preserve dirty local changes, and report conflicts instead of overwriting.
- Flow refreshes or proposes refreshing MCP/projected skill configuration for the local agent target and reports approval/default-tool settings needed for automation.
- Flow runs safe preflight checks for required runtimes/toolchains and reports actionable blockers, without running live PLexus/Pharo/Docker work unless an approved runtime profile exists.
- Flow produces a machine-local setup report that another agent can read before continuing work.

Dependencies:
- Depends on `dev-nexus:local-40` for the generic setup assistant service.
- Depends on `dev-nexus:local-39` for meta-project hosting configuration and auth profile references.
- Depends on `dev-nexus:local-38` for actor/remote guardrails when publishing from the new machine.

Boundaries:
- Do not overwrite existing dirty component source roots or host-local config.
- Do not copy secrets into shared project state.
- Do not launch live runtime services/images during baseline onboarding.

Verification:

Publication notes:

items.title: Slice component multi-tracker work tracking PRD into implementation items

items.title: Add dry-run work-item sync planner
items.description:

Source PRD: `docs/component-multi-tracker-prd.md`.

Goal: build a mutation-free sync planner that shows which selected source items would create, update, skip, or conflict with target tracker items.

- As a coordinator, I can see missing capabilities or credentials before attempting sync.

Acceptance criteria:

- A dry-run planner reads source and target trackers through provider capabilities and existing link records.
- Planner output includes creates, updates, skipped items, conflicting fields, missing provider capabilities, missing credentials, and policy blocks.
- Planner detects linked versus unlinked target items without creating provider objects.
- Planner treats provider capability gaps as planned skips or blockers, not silent partial success.
- CLI and MCP expose a dry-run sync plan with concise JSON suitable for agents.

Dependencies:
- Depends on `dev-nexus:local-44`, `dev-nexus:local-45`, and `dev-nexus:local-46`.

Boundaries:
- Dry-run only: no provider mutation, no link creation, and no external comments.
- Two-way sync remains out of scope.

Verification:

items.title: Execute one-way local-to-GitHub work-item sync with mocked providers

- DevNexus-generated comments include stable markers or fingerprints to prevent duplicate/echo comments.
- Execution records a sync run summary with created, updated, skipped, conflicted, and blocked counts plus provider links.

items.title: Choose first live multi-tracker dogfood sync policy
items.description:
Type: human-in-the-loop (HITL) policy decision slice
Readiness: blocked on provider/write policy and dogfood target choice.
Source PRD: `docs/component-multi-tracker-prd.md` open questions.

Goal: decide the first live dogfood sync policy before enabling real external provider mutation for multi-tracker sync.

Decision points:
- Should the first live target be GitHub Issues only, or GitHub Issues plus GitHub Project v2 board status?
- Should the dogfood project keep local as primary until bot identity and meta-project hosting guardrails are complete?
- Which item subset should sync first: `dogfood` ready items, coordination-only handoffs, or a smaller manual allowlist?
- Which comments should mirror externally: all DevNexus progress comments, only handoffs, only high-signal status transitions, or none initially?
- Which host-local auth profile or bot identity is allowed to create/update external issues?
- What quiet-period and no-repeat-ping rules apply to external waiting states?

Acceptance criteria:

- Confirm whether live writes are allowed or whether the first rollout remains draft-only.

items.title: Add portable project path resolution
items.description:
Type: DevNexus project config/path portability slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source finding: Mac setup exposed that dev-nexus.project.json currently stores Windows absolute component sourceRoot paths. A shared DevNexus meta project should be portable across Windows, macOS, and Linux without rewriting shared config for each host.

Goal: add a generic portable path model and resolver so shared project config can use one OS-neutral path style while DevNexus code and agent surfaces resolve it to host-local filesystem paths.

Design direction:
- Shared config should prefer portable path expressions, not raw OS absolute paths, for component sourceRoot, worktreesRoot, MCP/generated config paths, runtime state roots, and setup paths.
- Use POSIX-style separators in shared config and resolve them through a central DevNexus path resolver on each host.
- Support stable bases such as projectRoot, projectParent, home, and a configured sourcesRoot/workspacesRoot where appropriate.
- Keep legacy absolute string paths accepted for compatibility, but status/setup checks should flag them as non-portable when they block another OS.
- Host-local overrides may still exist for exceptional layouts, but normal Mac/Windows sharing should work from the same shared path expressions.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus has a typed path expression/resolution service with Windows, macOS, and Linux tests.
- Project config validation accepts portable path expressions or normalized portable strings for component source roots while preserving legacy absolute string compatibility.
- Project/component status, setup plan/check, worktree setup, automation context, MCP config refresh, and verification command cwd resolution use the central resolver instead of ad hoc path.resolve calls where project-config paths are involved.
- Setup assistant recommends portable shared paths for new projects and reports legacy absolute paths as portability warnings or blockers with migration guidance.
- Existing projects with absolute paths continue to work on the original host.
- Tests cover Windows and POSIX conversion, project-relative paths, home/source base paths, legacy absolute paths, and serialized JSON stability.

Boundaries:
- Do not store machine-specific secrets, SSH keys, tokens, or browser auth state in shared config.
- Do not hard-code this dogfood project's Windows or Mac layout into DevNexus core.
- Do not require all existing users to migrate before legacy configs continue working.

Verification:
- Focused path resolver and project config tests.
- Focused setup assistant/status/context tests proving a shared portable config resolves correctly on Windows and macOS fixtures.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.
- This should unblock the full new-machine setup flow tracked by dev-nexus:local-42.

items.title: Move automation agent profiles to portable host-local config
items.description:
Type: DevNexus automation/profile portability slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source finding: portable component source roots now unblock Mac setup, but this dogfood dev-nexus.project.json still stores Windows-specific Codex executable paths and --add-dir paths inside automation.agent.profiles. A Mac can run setup and component work manually, but DevNexus-managed automation launch from shared config remains host-specific.

Goal: separate portable automation profile intent from host-local executable/path material so the same shared meta project can launch coordinators on Windows or macOS without rewriting dev-nexus.project.json.

Acceptance criteria:
- Automation agent profiles can reference a host-local profile id or resolve command/path expressions through the central path resolver.
- Shared config no longer needs raw Windows Codex executable paths or component absolute --add-dir values.
- Host-local profile configuration stores executable paths, app-server endpoints, and OS-specific args outside shared project state.
- Agent launch context reports which host-local profile was selected and blocks clearly if it is missing.
- Existing Windows dogfood launch profile remains compatible through migration or host-local override.
- Tests cover Windows and macOS fixture profile resolution, missing profile blockers, and serialized shared config stability.

Boundaries:
- Do not store tokens, private keys, or browser auth state in shared config.
- Do not make DevNexus choose work; this only prepares the launcher infrastructure.

Verification:

- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Add extensible agent-provider MCP target support
items.description:
Type: agent-provider MCP projection slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) with current provider docs; no live credentials required.
Source finding: Mac onboarding exposed that DevNexus setup guidance must not assume Codex Desktop. Users may prefer OpenCode or another MCP-capable agent provider with a different project/session and config model.

Goal: make DevNexus agent-provider setup extensible beyond the current Codex/Claude concrete projection writers, starting with OpenCode if its documented MCP config model is stable enough.

Acceptance criteria:
- Agent MCP target configuration distinguishes provider identity, config path, config format/schema, project/session activation notes, and approval/tool-trust semantics where supported.
- `project mcp refresh` reports unsupported provider/config combinations as explicit capability gaps instead of Codex-specific assumptions or generic crashes.
- OpenCode support is implemented when official docs confirm the config shape; otherwise a documented custom-provider/manual-config path exists.
- Setup plan/check output remains provider-neutral and lists configured target agents plus exact generated or manual config files.
- Tests cover Codex, Claude, OpenCode or custom provider fixtures, unsupported provider warnings, and project/session setup-record readiness.

Boundaries:
- Do not edit provider global state, app databases, credentials, or private auth material.
- Do not assume every provider uses Codex TOML or Claude-style JSON.
- Keep DevNexus generic; provider-specific behavior belongs in target adapters.

Verification:
