- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Expose referenced planning docs in worker context
items.description:
Type: worker-context setup defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: during the 2026-05-17 parallel dogfood wave, the local-44 worker prompt referenced `docs/component-multi-tracker-prd.md` inside the prepared component worktree, but DevNexus worktree preparation only projected core context files and did not make referenced root docs available in the worktree. The worker had to report an input gap and continue from acceptance criteria until the coordinator sent the root PRD path manually.

Goal: make prepared worker contexts expose or resolve work-item referenced planning documents so agents do not receive nonexistent doc paths.

Acceptance criteria:
- Worktree preparation detects root-relative doc references from work-item descriptions and/or selected project docs and records them in `.dev-nexus/context/context.json` plus `briefing.md` as read-only inputs with absolute paths.
- Coordinator/worker briefing avoids implying that root docs exist inside the component worktree unless they are explicitly projected there.
- If a referenced planning file is missing, setup reports a clear capability/input gap before launch.
- Tests cover a work item referencing `docs/component-multi-tracker-prd.md` and prove the worker context includes the correct read-only root path.

Boundaries:
- Do not copy arbitrary project docs into source worktrees by default if read-only absolute references are enough.
- Preserve component worktree ownership: root docs are context, not worker write scope.

Verification:
- Focused worker context/worktree prepare tests.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Add active MCP visibility diagnostics for agent sessions
items.description:
Type: DevNexus setup/MCP diagnostics defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: after Codex was restarted on 2026-05-17, `.codex/config.toml` and `codex mcp list` showed `dev_nexus` and `dev_nexus_pharo` enabled, and direct stdio probes proved both servers initialize and list tools. However the active Codex tool surface in this thread still exposes PLexus/Pharo namespaces but not `mcp__dev_nexus` or `mcp__dev_nexus_pharo`. Setup check only reports a generic provider-session warning, so a coordinator can believe the root is ready while silently falling back to CLI.

Goal: make DevNexus onboarding/setup distinguish generated MCP config, server startability, provider config listing, and active-session tool visibility with actionable diagnostics.

Acceptance criteria:
- Setup check or a provider diagnostics command probes configured stdio/HTTP MCP servers for initialize/tools-list without requiring implementation work.
- For Codex, diagnostics can compare generated config, `codex mcp list` or configured CLI equivalent, and a recorded active-session confirmation step without relying on the fragile plain `codex` app alias.
- The check reports which expected namespaces/tools are configured, startable, provider-listed, and still unconfirmed in the active session.
- Guidance tells the user or coordinator exactly whether to restart/reopen the provider session, record the session, refresh MCP config, or fix a broken command.
- Tests cover configured-but-not-visible, command-not-startable, HTTP endpoint-unreachable, and all-good cases with mocked providers.

Evidence from this run:
- `.codex/config.toml` declares `dev_nexus`, `dev_nexus_pharo`, `plexus_project`, `pharo_launcher`, and `pharo`.
- Exact Codex CLI path can list MCP servers, but plain `codex mcp list` hits an access-denied app alias in this shell.
- Direct stdio probes report `dev-nexus@0.1.0` with 24 tools and `dev-nexus-pharo@0.1.0` with 25 tools.
- Active thread tools still lack the generic DevNexus MCP namespaces.

Verification:
- Focused setup/provider diagnostics tests.
- npm run check.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Harden npm dogfood package propagation and runtime installs
items.description:
Type: setup/package-install reliability defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: several dogfood setup and worker runs have depended on freshly published npm dogfood prereleases. Registry propagation or transient fetch failures caused confusing manual recovery: DevNexus-Pharo name-based install initially saw a missing packument after publish, a PLexus worker failed to fetch `@evref-bl/pharo-launcher-mcp` and left partial `node_modules`, and DevNexus alpha.9 install had to be retried after the dogfood tag became visible.

Goal: make DevNexus-owned setup/runtime package installation resilient and explicit so workers do not discover npm propagation or partial-install failures mid-task.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus setup/runtime package installation and publish-smoke guidance use bounded retry/backoff for npm packument/dist-tag visibility after a publish.
- Runtime package install preflight detects partial or stale `node_modules` state and either repairs it through an approved setup command or reports a clear blocker before launching workers.
- Worker context explains that package installation/fetch is setup-owned; workers should not run ad hoc npm install/npx fetches when dependencies are unavailable.
- Failure summaries distinguish registry propagation delay, network failure, missing package/version, and damaged local install state.
- Tests mock delayed npm visibility and partial runtime install directories without live registry access.

Verification:
- Focused setup/runtime package tests.
- npm run check.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Make local work-item store writes concurrency-safe
items.description:
Type: local work-tracker data-integrity defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: During the 2026-05-17 PLexus parallel worktree wave, parallel DevNexus local-provider mutations corrupted .dev-nexus/work-items/plexus.json. Two coordination/comment writes reused comment ids and the file ended with a duplicated JSON tail after the closing brace. Subsequent work-item updates and coordination integrate failed with: Unexpected non-whitespace character after JSON at position 72490. The coordinator repaired the file manually by removing only the duplicate suffix.

Goal:
Make local work-item stores safe under concurrent create, update, comment, and coordination handoff operations.

Acceptance criteria:
- Every local-provider mutation uses a per-store transactional write path: acquire lock or equivalent serialization, read fresh state, allocate ids under that lock, write to a temporary file, and atomically replace the store.
- Concurrent comments or updates against the same store produce distinct ids and preserve all records.
- DevNexus never appends or partially rewrites a store that fails JSON parsing.
- Parse failures include the store path, operation, and recovery guidance, without losing the original error detail.
- Tests simulate concurrent create/comment/update/handoff operations against the same local store and assert valid JSON plus preserved records.

Verification:
Run focused local work-tracker provider tests, coordination handoff tests that use the local provider, and npm run check.

items.title: Normalize component-qualified ids in coordination and worktree tools
items.description:
Type: component work-item routing defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: During the 2026-05-17 PLexus parallel wave, coordination handoff failed for component-qualified ids such as plexus:local-15 even when --component plexus was provided: Local work item not found. The same command succeeded only after switching to provider-local id local-15. DevNexus worktree prepare also accepted the component-qualified work item but produced worker context with a null work-item title.

Goal:
Make all DevNexus coordination and worktree tools route component-qualified work item ids consistently with work-item get/update/comment.

Acceptance criteria:
- coordination status, handoff, integrate, and request accept component-qualified ids with or without an explicit --component when the component can be resolved safely.
- worktree prepare resolves component-qualified work items to the owning component and includes the work-item title in generated context/briefing metadata.
- Error messages name the requested component, provider-local id, and configured tracker when lookup fails.
- Tests cover local provider items, component-qualified ids, provider-local ids with --component, and ambiguous/mismatched component errors.

Verification:
Run focused coordination CLI/MCP tests, worktree prepare tests, and npm run check.

items.title: Report coordination integration tracker parse failures actionably
items.description:
Type: coordination diagnostics defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK), after or alongside the local-provider concurrency fix
Source: During the 2026-05-17 PLexus integration, coordination integrate failed with a raw JSON.parse message after the local tracker store was corrupted. The command did not identify the store path, affected component, failing provider operation, or a safe recovery path.

Goal:
Make coordination integrate and related coordination readers degrade cleanly when tracker stores or handoff comments are malformed.

Acceptance criteria:
- coordination integrate reports component id, tracker id/provider, store path when local, and the failing read/parse stage for malformed tracker state.
- The command returns structured JSON with ok: false and actionable diagnostics instead of crashing with an uncaught parser message.
- Malformed handoff comments are skipped or reported individually without hiding valid handoffs from other work items.
- Tests cover malformed local stores, malformed handoff comment JSON blocks, and valid handoffs coexisting with bad records.

Verification:
Run focused coordination integrate diagnostics tests and npm run check.

items.title: Resolve Windows Codex coordinator command without host-specific shared config
items.description:
Type: automation infrastructure repair
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: heartbeat coordinator-loop target cycle target-cycle-coordinator-loop-20260517-t205231-721-z-1 failed before work selection because the configured Codex coordinator profile used bare `codex`, which Windows resolved to the WindowsApps alias and failed with `Acc?s refus?`; the user-local Codex Desktop binary runs correctly.

Goal: make shared DevNexus Codex coordinator profiles portable while avoiding Windows App Execution Alias failures.

Acceptance criteria:
- Shared project profiles can keep `command: codex` without encoding one machine's absolute path.
- On Windows, DevNexus resolves a Codex executor profile's bare `codex` command to the user-local Codex Desktop CLI when it exists, or uses an explicit host-local override when configured.
- The generated shell command quotes resolved command paths safely.
- Tests cover Windows local Codex command resolution and command-path quoting without requiring a real Codex install.
- The dogfood project runtime package is refreshed so `automation coordinator-loop` no longer fails with `Acc?s refus?` before coordinator selection.

Verification:
- Run focused agent profile command-resolution tests.
- Run `npm run check` in DevNexus when feasible.
- Smoke the dogfood coordinator launch path with a harmless command/profile check or a bounded coordinator-loop dry path that does not start unrelated implementation.

Publication notes:
- Publish DevNexus to origin/main and npm dogfood tag if verification passes, then refresh `.dev-nexus/runtime/npm-tools` in the dogfood root.

items.title: Make DevNexus-launched Git commands noninteractive
items.description:
Type: automation ergonomics defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: During a dogfood meta-repo rebase on 2026-05-17, `git rebase --continue` opened VS Code on `.git/COMMIT_EDITMSG` because the host global Git `core.editor` is VS Code with `--wait`. Agents should not rely on the human to close editors or complete interactive Git prompts.

Goal: make DevNexus-launched commands noninteractive for Git editor prompts by default.

Acceptance criteria:
- Agent launch environments set `GIT_EDITOR=true`, `GIT_SEQUENCE_EDITOR=true`, and `GIT_MERGE_AUTOEDIT=no` unless the caller already supplied explicit values.
- Command-executor environments use the same noninteractive Git defaults.
- Tests prove existing caller-provided values are preserved and defaults are added when absent.
- The change does not alter the user's global or repository Git configuration.

Verification:
- Focused automation environment tests.
- `npm run check`.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes; publish dogfood prerelease if needed by automation runtime.

local-18.body: Still blocked on live external posting policy. Recommended minimal unblock: default coordination_request to draft-only everywhere; allow live GitHub/GitLab/Jira comments only when the project config explicitly names the provider surface and target issue/PR/MR, with no auto-approval, no merge action, and no repeated pings.

External posting policy investigation update: prefer draft-only by default. If using GitHub as the shared tracker, live comments/status updates can be allowed only from a dedicated bot/GitHub App to named issues/PRs/projects; no auto-approval, merge, repeated pings, or writes outside the configured coordination repo/project.

User direction update: automation should not act as the user personal GitHub account. Prefer a visible bot identity, ideally via a GitHub App or least-privilege dedicated machine user in a custom private organization. Live writes should stay scoped to the coordination org/repo/project.

Brainstormed live external coordination policy notes and recorded them in `docs/shared-multi-host-coordination-prd.md`.

Key direction captured:
- provider actions should be capability-scoped: draft, post, assign, label, ping, review, merge, close, and publish are not the same permission.
- likely coordination events include error triage, solution agreement, option choice, ownership transfer, permission/runtime approval, PR/MR review, review response, CI ambiguity, integration conflicts, scope changes, API/config/dependency changes, release/rollback, stale waits, upstream dependency decisions, and human escalation.
- assignment is useful as an ownership/expected-responder signal, but should not be treated as approval by default.
- approval should come from provider-native review approval, configured label/comment/status transition, and recorded provider object/actor/timestamp evidence.
- silence should become waiting/timed_out/blocked, not implicit approval.

Remaining HITL decision: decide the first live-provider policy matrix before enabling external posting beyond draft/mocked flows.

Additional policy decision from user review:

- The coordinator can make most ordinary event-handling decisions by default, and dogfood experience will refine the policy over time.
- Every external coordination event should update the DevNexus project state machine: work-item status/labels, target-cycle facts, notes, continuation decisions, or publication gates.
- Assignment is not approval. Assignment means ownership or expected responder.
- Approval should be represented by explicit provider state transitions. For GitHub, prefer labels or GitHub Projects status/category moves for issue-level approval; for pull requests, require provider-native review approval plus required checks and DevNexus publication policy.

Recorded this in `docs/shared-multi-host-coordination-prd.md` and target-state. Remaining live-posting blockers are concrete provider mapping, credentials/test target, and rate-limit/ping defaults before non-draft external mutations.

local-62.body:
Completed and published.

Root cause:
- On Windows, bare `codex` resolved to the WindowsApps alias `C:\Program Files\WindowsApps\OpenAI.Codex_...\app\resources\codex.exe`.
- That alias fails from this host shell with `Acc?s refus?`.
- The user-local Codex Desktop binary `C:\Users\gabriel.darbord\AppData\Local\OpenAI\Codex\bin\codex.exe` runs successfully.

Fix:
- DevNexus now keeps shared profiles portable with `command: codex`.
- When resolving a Codex executor profile on Windows, DevNexus uses the user-local Codex Desktop CLI if present, or `DEV_NEXUS_CODEX_COMMAND` when explicitly set.
- Resolved command paths are shell-quoted, so paths with spaces are safe.
- The CLI test no longer assumes a host-invariant executable string.

Published source/package:
- DevNexus commit `8358ad8` pushed to `origin/main`.
- Published `@evref-bl/dev-nexus@0.1.0-alpha.13` on npm with the `dogfood` tag.
- Refreshed this dogfood root's local runtime install so `.dev-nexus/runtime/npm-tools/node_modules/dev-nexus` is alpha.13.

Verification:
- Focused: `npm test -- src/nexusAutomationAgentProfile.test.ts` passed.
- Full: `npm run check` passed in the implementation worktree and again in the publish checkout: 48 test files, 327 tests.
- Runtime smoke: installed package resolves the dogfood coordinator profile to `C:\Users\gabriel.darbord\AppData\Local\OpenAI\Codex\bin\codex.exe`, and the command runner executes that binary with `--version` successfully.
